coredata.dc:title: Self-assembled 3D hierarchical nanostructure of reduced GO nanosheets intercalated with CDs for high-rate supercapacitor electrodes
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Meiling
Han, Fangming
coredata.dc:description: A simple hydrothermal method for the preparation of 3D net-work hierarchical nanostructures consisting of carbon dots (CDs) intercalated between reduced graphene oxide (GO) nanosheets (denoted as CDs@rGONS) is demonstrated in this work. The incorporation of CDs as nano-spacers has effectively preventing aggregations of the rGONS, and at the same time greatly improved the reduction efficiency of the GO, both of which obviously enhance the electrochemical performance of the hierarchical nanostructures. Simple supercapacitors were assembled using the samples with different CDs/GO mass ratios, and it was proved that for the sample obtained with a CDs/GO mass ratio of 0.6 (denoted as CDs@rGONS0.6), a high specific capacitance of 239 F/g was obtained according to the CV curves. Moreover, a high energy density of 24 Wh/kg at 1 A/g and a high power density of 15.8 kW/kg at 10 A/g were obtained respectively, both of which are much higher than those without the presence of CDs. The good cycle stability (92.1% capacitance retention after 11500 cycles) of the sample was studied too. These excellent electrochemical performances proved their potential energy storage applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene nanosheets
Carbon dots
Supercapacitor

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic showing the fabrication of the CDs@rGONS hierarchical nanostructures and the assembling process of the simple supercapacitor.

a, b) Different magnifications of SEM images of the CDs@rGONS0.6 hierarchical nanostructures (the inset showing the photo of the CDs and GO mixed solution before and after hydrothermal treatment), c) and d) low magnification and high resolution lattice-resolved TEM images of the CDs@rGONS0.6, e) and f) Raman and XRD spectra of the pristine GO (red lines), RGO (green lines) and the CDs@rGONS0.6 (black lines) respectively. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
a) XPS whole survey spectra and high resolution b) C1s, c) O1s and d) N1s spectra of the pristine GO, the rGONS obtained via hydrothermal treatment of GO alone and the CDs@rGONS0.6, respectively.

a) CV diagrams of the simple supercapacitor obtained using the CDs@rGONS0.6 as electrode materials at different scan rates, b) galvanostatic charge/discharge curves at different current densities, c) Nyquist curve and d) cycling stability of the supercapacitor at 10 A/g. The inset in c) is the magnified Nyquist curve in the high frequency range, and that in d) shows the galvanostatic charge/discharge curves of the last 10 cycles.
a) Specific capacitance of the supercapacitor obtained using different electrode materials at different current densities, and b) Ragone plots of different samples.

head.ce:title: Self-assembled 3D hierarchical nanostructure of reduced GO nanosheets intercalated with CDs for high-rate supercapacitor electrodes

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Materials Physics, CAS Center for Excellence in Nanoscience, and Anhui Key Laboratory of Nanomaterials and Nanotechnology, Institute of Solid State Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Hefei, 230031, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CAS Center for Excellence in Nanoscience, and Anhui Key Laboratory of Nanomaterials and Nanotechnology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A simple hydrothermal method for the preparation of 3D net-work hierarchical nanostructures consisting of carbon dots (CDs) intercalated between reduced graphene oxide (GO) nanosheets (denoted as CDs@rGONS) is demonstrated in this work. The incorporation of CDs as nano-spacers has effectively preventing aggregations of the rGONS, and at the same time greatly improved the reduction efficiency of the GO, both of which obviously enhance the electrochemical performance of the hierarchical nanostructures. Simple supercapacitors were assembled using the samples with different CDs/GO mass ratios, and it was proved that for the sample obtained with a CDs/GO mass ratio of 0.6 (denoted as CDs@rGONS0.6), a high specific capacitance of 239 F/g was obtained according to the CV curves. Moreover, a high energy density of 24 Wh/kg at 1 A/g and a high power density of 15.8 kW/kg at 10 A/g were obtained respectively, both of which are much higher than those without the presence of CDs. The good cycle stability (92.1% capacitance retention after 11500 cycles) of the sample was studied too. These excellent electrochemical performances proved their potential energy storage applications.

ce:section.ce:para: With the ever increasing demand for novel energy storage systems in electrical device areas, supercapacitors have drawn considerable attention as a result of their high power density, fast charge–discharge rate and long cycling life [1]. Compared to batteries, supercapacitors can provide a much higher power density, longer cycle-life and safer energy storage mechanism, due to their simple charging circuit and physical energy storage mechanism [2]. So far, low energy density and high production costs are deemed to be the two major challenges for future development of supercapacitors. Electrochemical double layer capacitors (EDLCs) that usually use low cost carbons as active materials, have high operation stability and are the most studied supercapacitors at present [3]. This type of supercapacitors store electrical energy by electrostatic accumulation of charges on the surface of the electric double-layer of the electrode materials. Thus for a high-performance supercapacitor with high specific capacitance and large rate capability, both sufficiently high specific surface area and good electrical conductivity of the electrode materials are needed [3].

Graphene, as a one-atom graphite sheet, has both ultra-high specific surface area and excellent electrical conductivity [4]. And the potential of graphene being used for EDLCs electrode materials, has been extensively demonstrated [4–9]. However, in most of those reports, high specific capacitances can only be achieved at low current densities, and this seriously limits their applications. The reason for this should be that, limited pores induced by the facile restack of the graphene sheets somewhat restricted the access of electrolyte ions to the inner channels among graphene sheets [4]. Therefore, it is crucial to prepare less agglomerated graphene-based self-supported materials for supercapacitor applications. It was reported that, hierarchical graphene nanostructures that comprises other different structural dimensions (0D or 1D) can greatly improve EDLC performance by their synergistic effects [10]. Therefore, recent advances in graphene-based EDLCs electrode materials have been focused on the fabrication of hierarchical nanostructures with various types of 0D carbon materials (carbon spheres [10], carbon black particles [11] and mesoporous carbon spheres [3]) and 1D CNTs [12,13].

Herein, in this report we present a hydrothermal self-assembly method to prepare inner-connected hierarchical nanostructures including both reduced graphene oxide nanosheets (rGONS) and 0 D carbon dots (CDs) (denoted as CDs@rGONS). As is known that, the loss of oxygen-containing groups during the reduction process of graphene oxide leads to agglomeration, as a result of the reduced electrostatic repulsion. The incorporation of CDs acting as nano-spacers, can partially inhibits agglomeration of the graphene sheets and improve the electrolyte ions penetration into the inner part of the material. Most importantly, our results prove that the incorporation of CDs to CDs@rGONS has greatly improved the reduction efficiency of GO, which effectively enhanced the conductivity and electrochemical performances of the CDs@rGONS. Detailed fabrication process is schematically shown in Fig. 1 . The hierarchical nanostructure of CDs@rGONS is obtained by the simple hydrothermal reduction of commercial graphene oxide and CDs at 180 °C for 3 h, followed by cryodesiccation. The material obtained with a CDs/GO mass ratio of x is denoted as CDs@rGONSx. Then the CDs@rGONSx was assembled to simple supercapacitors to demonstrate their capacitance performances as shown in Fig. 1. Especially, for the CDs@rGONS0.6, the as-fabricated supercapacitor shows a high specific capacitance of 239 F/g at a voltage scan rate of 5 mV/s, and a high specific capacitance of 270 F/g was calculated based on the galvanostatic charge-discharge method at 1 A/g. An outstanding energy density of 24 Wh/kg was obtained at a current density of 1 A/g, and a high power density of 15.8 kW/kg was obtained at a current density of 10 A/g. Furthermore, the supercapacitor assembled by this method exhibited good rate capability and cycling stability, and 92.1% capacitance retention is achieved after 11500 cycles at 10 A/g. Thus the active material prepared by our method has remarkable performance in supercapacitor application. Furthermore, electrode materials fabricated with different CDs/GO mass ratios were compared. And our experiments prove that the CDs@rGONS0.6 shows the best electrochemical performances compared to CDs@rGONS0.2 and CDs@rGONS1, and this can be attributed to its much looser structure compared to CDs@rGONS0.2 and its much higher conductivity than that of CDs@rGONS1. As CDs can be easily obtained via a simple hydrothermal treatment of various biomass by-products including grass, pomelo peel, peanut shells and bagasse with high productivity [14–17], the electrode material prepared in this work has the superiority of simplicity and low production costs.

2 mg/mL GO in aqueous solution was used as purchased without further treatments. The CDs were prepared by hydrothermal treatment of 1.2 g crushed grass in 50 ml de-ionized water solution at 200 °C for 5 h, followed by centrifugation, dialysis and freeze-drying. Then the obtained CDs powder was re-dispersed in de-ionized water to form a 2 mg/ml solution for further use. As for the preparation of the CDs@rGONSx, 1 ml commercial GO solution (2 mg/ml) with x ml CDs solution (2 mg/mL) was put into an autoclave after being mixed together, and heated at 180 °C for 3 h. After cooled to room temperature, the sample was freeze-dried for further characterization and use. The morphologies and structures of the CDs@rGONS0.6 hierarchical nanostructures were characterized by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). Raman spectra were measured on a confocal microprobe Raman spectrometer (RenishawInvia) with excitation wavelength of 532 nm. X-ray diffraction measurement was obtained using a D8 Focus instrument. X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were measured using a photoelectron spectrometer.

The as-obtained freeze-dried CDs@rGONS0.6 cylindrical sample with a diameter of about 0.4 cm was cut into paper-thin slices. As shown in Fig. 1, a sandwiched layered structure was assembled using two slices (about 0.3 mg each) with a separator between the active materials, and two Pt foils were used as the current collectors. Then a simple supercapacitor consisting two symmetric working electrodes was assembled. The simple supercapacitor cell was immersed in 1 M H2SO4 electrolyte aqueous solution overnight before electrochemical measurements. The electrochemical and capacitive performances of the samples were investigated by cyclic voltammetry (CV), electrochemical impedance spectroscopy and galvanostatic charge-discharge curves. And all the electrochemical measurements were conducted on an electrochemical workstation (Zahner Zennium).

Different magnifications of SEM images of the CDs@rGONS with CDs/GO mass ratio of 0.6 (denoted as CDs@rGONS0.6) are shown in Fig. 2 a and b, which indicate a strong cross-linked 3D network macrostructure including flexible and crumpled graphene nanosheets, similar to but fluffier than previous reports [4,17–20]. The existence of CDs on the surface of the graphene nanosheets can be demonstrated by TEM images of the CDs@rGONS0.6 (Fig. 2c and d). From Fig. 2c, it can be seen that CDs with diameters ranging from 2 to 5 nm are uniformly dispersed on one graphene nanosheet. Furthermore, high resolution lattice-resolved TEM image of the CDs@rGONS0.6 reveals interplaner spacing of 0.32 nm for the CDs, corresponding to (1120) lattice plane of graphitic carbon. As shown in Fig. 2e, all the Raman spectra of pristine GO, RGO and the CDs@rGONS0.6 show G and D bands at 1590 and 1340 cm−1 respectively. Compared to pristine GO and RGO, the Raman spectrum of CDs@rGONS0.6 has an enhanced I
D
/I
G , implying increased defect density due to hydrothermal reduction and the interaction between CDs and rGONS [18]. Fig. 2f shows the X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) pattern of the GO (red line), RGO (green line) and the CDs@rGONS0.6 (black line) respectively. The pattern of the CDs@rGONS0.6 exhibits a broad peak of 2θ centered at 23.4° compared to that of 29.7° for GO and 25.1° for RGO, indicating that CDs@rGONS0.6 shows a larger graphitic interplaner spacing (002) of 3.78 Å than that of GO and RGO according to 2dsinθ = λ, which is higher than that of graphite (3.36 Å) [4,11,17]. This result proves that, the presence of CDs as nano-spacers has effectively reduced rGONS aggregation in this novel hierarchical structures. The surface chemistry of the pristine GO, the rGONS obtained via the simple hydrothermal treatment of GO solution alone, and the CDs@rGONS0.6 is analysed using XPS (see Fig. 3 ). For the pristine GO, C1s XPS spectrum shows a strong peak corresponding to oxygen-containing functionalities (286.6 eV, black line in Fig. 3b). For the rGONS, this peak becomes much weaker (red line in Fig. 3b), but there still exhibits considerable degree of oxidation. However, for the C1s XPS spectrum of the CDs@rGONS0.6, these oxygen-containing functionalities almost disappeared (blue line in Fig. 3b), confirming that they are completely reducted. This result can also be proved by the O1s XPS spectra shown in Fig. 3c (the O1s peak disappeared for the CDs@rGONS0.6). Furthermore, the XPS results reveal significant oxygen contents of 33.1%, 18.54% and 2.88% for the pristine GO, the rGONS and the CDs@rGONS0.6, respectively. However, the XPS analysis for the CDs indicates an oxygen content of 24.7%. All of the above-mentioned results prove that, the presence of CDs has greatly improved the chemical reduction efficiency of GO, and the reducibility of CDs has already been reported by previous work [21,22]. This high chemical reduction efficiency of GO results in reduced number of oxygenated groups, thus greatly enhances the conductivity of the CDs@rGONS0.6 [4]. Moreover, for the CDs@rGONS0.6, the much larger amount of gaseous byproducts (such as CO2 and CO) formed during GO reduction process, leads to more nanosized pores as a result of the blasting of the formed blisters [23]. These nano-sized pores are essential for the facile access of the electrolyte ions to the inner part of the electrode materials. However, for CDs@rGONSx with a X value of X > 0.6, too many pores can be formed in the inner part of the materials, which may greatly affect the conductivity of the materials and induce decreased specific capacitance. Thus CDs@rGONS0.6 sample shows the largest specific capacitance under our present experimental conditions. And it should be noted that the incorporation of CDs has induced a considerable N-doping of the CDs@rGONS0.6. As shown in Fig. 3d, with a nitrogen content of 2.96%, the charge state density and the wettability of the CDs@rGONS0.6 as electrode materials can be greatly improved [24,25], which can facilitate the penetration of the aqueous electrolyte ions to the inner region of the electrode [20].

The capacitance performance of the as-assembled simple supercapacitor was evaluated using CV curves (Fig. 4 a) and galvanostatic charge-discharge tests (Fig. 4b). As shown in Fig. 4a, CV curves of the supercapacitor using the CDs@rGONS0.6 as electrode materials show quasi-rectangular shapes in the scan range of 0–0.8 V at various scan rates, indicating an excellent electric double layer capacitance performance even at a high scan rate of 200 mV/s. The specific capacitance of the CDs@rGONS0.6 estimated from the CV curves are 239 F/g at 5 mV/s and 121 F/g at 200 mV/s, respectively. The linear profile and symmetric triangular shapes of the galvanostatic charge-discharge curves shown in Fig. 4b indicate almost ideal capacitive behavior. And specific capacitance obtained using the galvanostatic charge-discharge curves are 270 and 140 F/g at 1 and 10 A/g respectively, comparable to those obtained using the CV curves. It should be illustrated that, the as-contained N atoms of the CDs@rGONS0.6 may pay additional roles in this high specific capacitance via their pseudo-capacitive contribution [26]. Fig. 4c shows the Nyquist curve of the CDs@rGONS0.6 based supercapacitor with well-defined semi-circle at high frequency range and nearly vertical straight line over the low frequency range, indicating the rapid electrolyte ions transport through the 3D network macrostructures [24,27]. An equivalent series resistance of 0.87 Ω (the intercept of the plot with the real impedance axis [10], see inset in Fig. 4c) was obtained, proving excellent conductivity of the sample [28]. And an interfacial charge-transfer resistance of 9.7 Ω was evaluated (the diameter of the semi-circle [27]). The cycling stability of the sample was studied through galvanostatic charge-discharge at a current density of 10 A/g. As shown in Fig. 4d, the as-assembled simple supercapacitor shows a specific capacitance retention of about 92.1% after 11500 cycles, indicating an excellent cycling stability.

In order to study the influence of CDs content on the capacitance performance of the electrode materials, samples with 0, 0.2 and 1 CDs/GO mass ratio were prepared (denoted as rGONS, CDs@rGONS0.2 and CDs@rGONS1 respectively) and their specific capacitance were compared with CDs@rGONS0.6. It can be seen from Fig. 5a that, with the incorporation of CDs, the specific capacitance of the sample has obviously improved compared to rGONS alone, and the CDs@rGONS0.6 shows a much larger specific capacitance than CDs@rGONS0.2. However, with the further increase of CDs content, the specific capacitance decreased (see red and blue lines in Fig. 5). As shown in Fig. 5a, the specific capacitance of all samples decreased with current densities, as a result of the limited movement of the electrolyte ions induced by diffusion at high scan rates and the limited outer active surface that can be utilized for charge storage, which are related to the number of micropores on the surface and the interfacial charge-transfer resistance of the electrode material [24,27,29–32]. However, the CDs@rGONS0.6 still shows a much higher capacitance of 140 F/g even at a current density of 10 A/g. The specific capacitance of different samples as a function of potential at different scan rates can be found in Fig. S1 in the supporting information, which further proves the much higher capacitance of the CDs@rGONS0.6 compared to other samples. Furthermore, the energy density and power density of all the four samples were calculated based on
在此处键入公式。 e
E =
□ □
samples
are
calculated
based
on
E =
1 2
C
(
∆ V )
2
a n d
P =
E
∆ t
where C is the specific capacitance of the samples obtained from the CV curves, ΔV is the potential window (0.8 V) and Δt is discharge time. As shown in the Ragone plots in Fig. 5b, CDs@rGONS0.2, CDs@rGONS0.6 and CDs@rGONS1 all show higher power density and energy density than other graphene-based hierarchical nanostructures [3,4,10,11,33]. Especially, the CDs@rGONS0.6 delivers a high power density of 15.8 kW/kg at 10 A/g (with power density of 1.6 kW/kg) and a high energy density of 24 Wh/kg at 1 A/g (with energy density of 12.4 Wh/kg), proving their excellent electrochemical performances. Outstanding energy densities of 20.8, 17.8, 16, 14.2 and 12.4 Wh/kg were obtained with current densities of 2, 4, 6 and 8 A/g respectively. Last but not least, the fabrication method of the CDs@rGONS0.6 is economic, environmentally friendly and easy to scale up, which is important for practical energy storage applications.

A 3D inner-connected hierarchical nanostructure based-on CDs intercalated between reduced graphene oxide nano-sheets (CDs@rGONS) was prepared. The existence of CDs as nano-spacers not only effectively reduces aggregations of rGONS, but also improves reduction efficiency of pristine GO, resulting in larger specific surface area and higher conductivity of the CDs@rGONS, both of which greatly enhance the electrochemical performance of the CDs@rGONS as supercapacitor electrode materials. Especially, for the CDs@rGONS0.6 (obtained with a CDs/GO mass ratio of 0.6), a high specific capacitance of 239 F/g, energy density of 24 Wh/kg and power density of 15.8 kW/kg were obtained respectively. And a capacitance retention of 92.1% was obtained after 11500 cycles at 10 A/g. All of the above results are induced by the low equivalent series impedance and the rapid ions diffusion induced by the hierarchical porous structures, as a result of the CDs incorporation.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21307138 and 51372247).

ce:section.ce:para: The following is the supplementary data related to this article:

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.jallcom.2017.08.229.
